input_summary: Real-time payment flow telemetry across all active corridors
finding_summary: Anomalous failure pattern detected: 47 payment timeouts in 90 seconds across US→GB, US→JP, US→SG corridors. Failure rate jumped from 0.1% to 34.2%. Pattern signature: cascading timeout with exponential queue growth.
narrative: I detected a multi-corridor anomaly at 14:23 UTC. The payment failure rate surged from baseline 0.1% to 34.2% within 90 seconds — well beyond normal variance. Three corridors are simultaneously affected, which rules out a single-point network issue. The pattern fingerprint matches a cascading upstream service failure. Escalating to Correlator Agent for root cause analysis.

input_summary: 47 failed payment events + infrastructure telemetry from Sentinel
finding_summary: Root cause: Sanctions screening microservice p99 latency at 14.2s (SLA: 3s). The legacy COBOL adapter interprets timeouts as soft failures and retries 3x per message, creating a feedback loop that amplifies queue depth 4x.
narrative: Cross-referencing payment failure traces with infrastructure metrics reveals the root cause chain: the sanctions screening service hit a latency spike (14.2s p99), likely due to a stale connection pool or upstream dependency. The critical amplifier is the legacy COBOL bridge — it treats timeouts as retryable errors and creates 3 additional messages per failure, turning a linear problem into exponential queue growth.
staticNarrative: Cross-referencing payment failure traces with infrastructure metrics reveals the root cause chain: the sanctions screening service hit a latency spike (14.2s p99), likely due to a stale connection pool or upstream dependency. The critical amplifier is the legacy COBOL bridge — it treats timeouts as retryable errors and creates 3 additional messages per failure, turning a linear problem into exponential queue growth.
businessSignal: Root cause isolated — sanctions service latency spike amplified by legacy adapter retry storm

input_summary: 2,847 log entries from payment gateway, sanctions service, and MQ broker
finding_summary: Semantic clustering identified 4 distinct log clusters: (1) Sanctions timeout errors (38%), (2) Legacy adapter retry noise (41%), (3) Queue overflow warnings (15%), (4) FX rate refresh failures (6% — secondary symptom).
narrative: I processed 2,847 log entries using semantic clustering. The dominant signal is sanctions timeout errors (38%) amplified by legacy adapter retries (41%). The retry noise is masking the real problem — in a traditional SOC, analysts would spend hours filtering retry logs before reaching the sanctions root cause. I've also flagged a secondary FX rate refresh failure (6%) that appeared as a downstream consequence.

input_summary: Recovery execution results + payment settlement confirmations
finding_summary: Recovery verified: 139/142 payments successfully processed. 3 payments require manual review (data quality issues pre-existing). STP rate restored to 98%. Total MTTR: 11 minutes (vs 90 min manual baseline). $824M recovered. FX window met with 18 minutes to spare.
narrative: Final verification complete. 139 out of 142 payments have been successfully processed and confirmed settled. Three payments flagged for manual review due to pre-existing data quality issues unrelated to the incident. The STP rate has been restored to 98%. Full audit trail generated for compliance.
